Title: MedDP: A Graph-based Large Language Disease Prediction Model
Abstract: <jats:p>The Disease Prediction (DP) task extracts diseases and symptoms from Electronic Health Records (EHR), predicting potential diseases and providing follow-up suggestions. LLM-based systems in healthcare have demonstrated remarkable capabilities. However, existing prediction models rely on a large amount of labeled high-quality data, yet fail to provide precise and interpretable prediction results. In this paper, we propose a graph-based general framework that extracts structured graphs from unstructured data to address these challenges. The MedDP model constructs a heterogeneous knowledge graph composed of symptoms and diseases and carries out chain-reasoning disease prediction on this graph. Evaluations on multiple datasets demonstrate that the MedDP model can outperform its competitors, which achieves F-score higher than 0.65 on million-scale datasets with different granularities and a Precision of 0.81 on the Q&A task; Moreover, the ablation experiment and scenario experiment have demonstrated the generalization and portability of MedDP. ??/jats:p>

Title: Physical Functioning, Perceived Disability, and Depressive Symptoms in Adults with Arthritis
Abstract: <jats:p>This study investigated how physical functioning and perceived disability are related to depressive symptoms in adults with arthritis (<mml:math xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" id="M1"><mml:mi>n</mml:mi><mml:mo>=</mml:mo><mml:mn>401</mml:mn></mml:math>). Participants self-reported depressive symptoms and disability. Objective measures of physical functioning included the 30-second chair stand test, 6-minute walk test, gait speed, balance, grip strength, and the seated reach test. Separate quantile regression models tested associations between each functional measure and depressive symptoms, controlling for age, gender, race, BMI, self-reported health status, and arthritis medication use. The association between perceived disability and depressive symptoms was also tested. Participants averaged<mml:math xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" id="M2"><mml:mn>56.3</mml:mn><mml:mo>?</mml:mo><mml:mn>10.7</mml:mn></mml:math>years; 85.8% were women; 64.3% were white. Lower distance in the 6-minute walk test, fewer chair stands, slower gait speed, and greater perceived disability were associated with greater depressive symptoms in unadjusted models (<mml:math xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" id="M3"><mml:mi>Ps</mml:mi><mml:mo><</mml:mo><mml:mo>0.</mml:mo><mml:mn>05</mml:mn></mml:math>). Fewer chair stands and greater perceived disability were associated with more depressive symptoms in adjusted models (<mml:math xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" id="M4"><mml:mi>Ps</mml:mi><mml:mo><</mml:mo><mml:mo>0.</mml:mo><mml:mn>05</mml:mn></mml:math>). Balance, grip strength, and seated reach were not related to depressive symptoms. The perception of being disabled was more strongly associated with depressive symptoms than reduced physical functioning. To reduce the risk of depression in arthritic populations, it may be critical to not only address physical symptoms but also to emphasize coping skills and arthritis self-efficacy.</jats:p>

Title: Public profiles in Alzheimer?? disease stigma: Unsupported, discriminated, and alone
Abstract:
<jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Introduction:</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Public stigma of Alzheimer?? disease (AD) can delay help-seeking and be a barrier to research participation. This study aimed to understand what, if any, patterns exist among aspects of AD stigma. Knowing this may advance opportunities to reduce AD stigma.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Methods:</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>
                      Adult respondents (
                      <jats:italic toggle="yes">N</jats:italic>
                      ????17) read a vignette about a man with mild stage AD dementia. Afterward, respondents answered the modified Family Stigma in Alzheimer?? Disease Scale (FS-ADS), which measures:
                      <jats:italic toggle="yes">Structural Discrimination, Negative Severity Attributions, Negative Aesthetic Attributions, Antipathy, Supportiveness, Pity</jats:italic>
                      , and
                      <jats:italic toggle="yes">Social Distance</jats:italic>
                      . In this correlational study, latent class analyses were used to derive response profiles. Regression models were used to assess correspondence of personal characteristics with profile membership.
                    </jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Results:</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>
                      Three profiles emerged from latent class analysis of four FS-ADS domains of:
                      <jats:italic toggle="yes">Structural Discrimination, Negative Severity Attributions, Supportiveness</jats:italic>
                      , and
                      <jats:italic toggle="yes">Social Distance.</jats:italic>
                      Two profiles characterized about 66.8% of respondents and were similar except for one distinguishing quality: beliefs that a person with AD would receive support from others. Additionally, membership in the ??xpecting higher support??profile was associated with identifying as White and having lower educational attainment, while membership in the ??xpecting lower support??profile was associated with relatively higher educational attainment.
                    </jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Conclusions:</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Beliefs about a lack of support, worries about discrimination, and expectations of social distance may depict a topic cluster to leverage in public messaging campaigns intended to reduce AD stigma. In doing so, our findings suggest it will be essential to consider the age and education level of the intended audience.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>

Title: Public-Private Partnership Options toward Achieving Universal Health Coverage in the Philippine Setting
Abstract: <jats:p>Public-private partnerships (PPPs) are increasingly being utilized to meet development goals of many countries and there is much potential for PPPs to help address health inequities and advance universal health care (UHC). In support of these efforts, the paper defines PPPs and their role in the health system and proposes health PPP options that would support the pursuit of UHC.The objectives of this paper are to define what PPPs are and their application in the health sector. The paper presents representative case studies or models of PPP practices in health. Furthermore, it aims to provide a methodology for decisionmakers and stakeholders in developing health PPPs.</jats:p>

Title: Scanning for Wellness: QR Code Strategies to Promote Public Health Programs
Abstract: <jats:p>This article discusses the importance of effective communication tools in public health, highlighting innovations like Quick Response (QR) codes and QR wallet reference cards (QR cards) for enhancing outreach and education. QR codes are scannable barcodes that link to digital content. QR cards are compact cards, similar to business cards, with codes that lead to relevant health information. To our knowledge, there is little published literature on using QR codes and cards for public health programs and health communication outside of health care clinics and education settings. The North Carolina Department of Health and Human Services, Division of Public Health, Community and Clinical Connections for Prevention and Health Branch has successfully implemented QR codes in various public health programs, particularly in diabetes management and nutrition, physical activity, and obesity initiatives. Key lessons learned include using reputable QR code generators, ensuring visibility and scanability of the codes, testing links before use, providing clear calls to action, and considering dynamic versus static codes based on needs. QR codes can be leveraged in public health practice for program promotion, evaluation sharing, and community resource accessibility. However, limitations such as smartphone dependency among some populations should be acknowledged. In conclusion, while QR codes are a simple tool, they hold significant potential for improving public health communication. Research on QR code use in public health settings could help inform best practices for public health programs and health promotion across different contexts.</jats:p>
